steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Cross-Text Connections question. The two authors disagree on historical accuracy (Text 1 treats the ride as real; Text 2 doubts it exists), so any point of agreement must come from something both texts explicitly state. The correct answer must be a claim both authors actually make — not an inference only one of them supports.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Ludington was crucial to the outcome of the Revolutionary War: Text 1 calls her a hero of the Revolution but never says she was crucial to its outcome. Text 2 focuses on the lack of evidence for her ride. Neither author makes this claim. ✗
  • Choice B — Historians have confirmed which route she took: this directly contradicts Text 2, which states there is no strong historical record of the ride at all. ✗
  • Choice C — Ludington was likely not a real person: Text 2 only questions the ride, not Ludington’s existence. Neither text suggests she was fictional. ✗
  • Choice D — Many people have come to admire the story of Ludington’s ride: Text 1 says she has been “widely celebrated, inspiring postage stamps, statues, and even children’s TV series.” Text 2 says “Ludington is revered by Americans today.” Both authors explicitly acknowledge her widespread popularity. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
